title: Asking ChatGPT if you’re a jerk could make you more of one
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Volcanoes may not erupt how scientists thought, cooperative birthing in sperm whales, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
27 March 2026
Today’s What We’re Enjoying features a review of Project Hail Mary from News from Science intern Perri Thaler. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including unprecedented footage of a sperm whale’s birth and how volcanologists may have gotten eruptions all wrong.
Artificial Intelligence  |  Science
Asking ChatGPT if you’re a jerk could make you more of one
For decades, people have turned to Reddit’s AITA (“am I the a—hole?”) forum for a moral gut-check. They lied to a friend, for example, feel justified, and ask for judgment. The commenters might push back—totally TA. But according to new research published in Science, a chatbot would most likely defend the poster.

When researchers fed large language models real interpersonal conflict scenarios drawn from Reddit and compared their responses to human judgments, the models were systematically more likely to agree with users’ perspectives than other people, validating behavior even when community consensus judged the user to be in the wrong. As co-author Myra Cheng told Sarah Crespi on the Science Podcast, AI responses were “ roughly 50% more affirming than people.”

In follow-up experiments, participants who received affirming AI feedback became more convinced of their own correctness and less willing to repair relationships. Cheng noted on the podcast that users were “much less likely to apologize [and] much less likely to want to change anything about themselves.” A deleterious feedback loop thus formed: Participants rated the most supportive responses as more helpful and trustworthy, making them more likely to rely on those systems again.

In a related Science Perspective, psychologist Anat Perry argues that this dynamic may erode a critical feature of human relationships: disagreement. Social learning depends on being challenged, corrected, and sometimes even proven wrong. Systems optimized for agreement, she explained, risk removing “the very social friction through which accountability, perspective-taking, and moral growth ordinarily unfold.”
Read the Science Paper  | Listen to the SCIENCE PODCAST
Read the Perspective
Animals  |  Science
Whale done teamwork
A team of adults helps a newborn calf to the surface.  © Project CETI
In July 2023, researchers on a whale watching expedition in the Caribbean encountered an unusual scene: 11 sperm whales clustered tightly at the water’s surface. The animals began inching closer and closer together in the waters off the island of Dominica, with their attention turned to one individual, until a burst of blood tinted the water. At first, the scientists—part of Project CETI, a nonprofit that studies how sperm whales interact—thought the animal had been attacked. But then they saw a tiny tail pop out of the water.

The researchers quickly realized they had in fact observed a group of whales helping a calf take its first breath. Even more fortuitous, they had videoed it from a drone—overhead footage that allowed the scientists to meticulously analyze the individual whales’ behavior before, during, and after the delivery. Those analyses revealed whales from different family branches can work together to support a newborn calf . “This observation confirms the suspected social complexity of whales and suggests that modern observational tools are likely to continue to reveal the secrets of other species that are difficult to study,” Science Editor Sacha Vignieri wrote in her editor’s summary.

The findings “are incredibly exciting,” said physiologist Jeremy Goldbogen, who was not involved in the work. “They represent just the tip of the iceberg.”
read the Science paper
Watch the Footage
VUlcanology  |  News from Science
Bubble, bubble, toil and trouble
For half a century, scientists have roughly agreed on a major accelerator of volcanic eruptions: bubbles. But what happens when those bubbles disappear?

As fresh magma enters a volcanic system from Earth’s high-pressure depths, the pressure subsides, and bubbles form like the fizz of an uncorked champagne bottle. Scientists have thought all those extra bubbles add volume to the system, building up pressure until an explosion.

To investigate further, researchers analyzed rocks from Japan’s Aso volcano that showed bubbles strangely disappearing during a major eruption 86,000 years ago. When they matched a model to their rock samples, it turned the bubbly mechanism on its head . Fizz, they propose, makes the magma squishy and able to adjust to tight spaces without causing too much pressure. But when the bubbles dissolve back into the magma, the system turns incompressible, the magma can’t squish, and the pressure ratchets up to accelerate an eruption.

“I’m a scientist [who’s] been around for 100 years,” joked geologist Phil Shane to ScienceAdviser. “It is quite a new idea about what happens to gases at the last minute.”
Read the full story
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what's possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
What we’re Enjoying
The Elusive Body
Alexandra Sifferlin
Today, doctors can treat sundry diseases that were death sentences a hundred years ago. But a clinician can’t cure a patient’s ailment if they cannot identify it. In The Elusive Body, journalist Alexandra Sifferlin delves into the millions of people with unnamed illnesses thanks to the “diagnosis crisis” in American medicine. “The book’s subject matter could easily lend itself to despair,” writes reviewer Lucy Tu. “Yet The Elusive Body ultimately resists both indictment and fatalism. If diagnostic failure is systemic, Sifferlin suggests, so too might be its repair. And in that possibility, she finds cautious, hard-won hope.”
Read the full review
Original Sin
Kathryn Paige Harden
A lot of who we are is written in our genes. But just how much of what we do is coded in DNA? In both her lab and her newest book, Original Sin, behavioral geneticist Kathryn Paige Harden explores the science and reflects on the potential medical and societal implications of those findings. “Original Sin is an ambitious, compact, and often moving contribution to the literature at the intersection of genetics and ethics,” writes reviewer Michael Goldman. “The book should serve as a nucleation point for broad, intense, and enlightening discussion.”
Read the full review
Project Hail Mary
Phil Lord and Christopher Miller, Directors
I went into Project Hail Mary expecting a straight-up sci-fi movie. And while it delivers on that front with clever problem-solving and a respect for scientific thinking, it surprised me by feeling much more like a buddy comedy. Compared to the book, the film trims some of the denser scientific explanations and internal monologue, streamlining the science without losing the spirit of curiosity. It’s still central, but it feels lighter and more conversational. What really stands out is how the story balances high-stakes research with humor and warmth. The central dynamic brings a playful energy that keeps all the technical material engaging. It’s rare to see molecular biology and orbital mechanics coexist with moments that made me laugh, and one or two that made me tear up. It’s smart, charming, and fun—I give it four stars out of five. Would recommend!
 —Perri Thaler, Intern, News from Science
Read the full review  |  Read the  Science Q&A WITH an Astronomer
Be sure to check out all the reviews in our Books et al. section.
podcast
Resurrection plants, Project Hail Mary, and the trouble with sycophantic AI
By Sarah Crespi, Martin Enserink   |   26 March 2025
On this week’s show: Advice from overly nice chatbots gives users too much confidence, plants provide lessons in surviving extreme water deprivation, and an astrophysicist weighs in on an out-of-this-world buddy movie
Listen Now
Et Cetera
A lifetime of stress
Scientists have long thought the unusual and self-destructive actions laboratory monkeys sometimes engage in were the result of a recent stressful experience such as being removed from a larger group or an experiment that caused pain or distress. But a study finds these abnormal repetitive behaviors can trace back years and may be the cumulative result of multiple stressors over a monkey’s lifetime. “This is a pretty solid piece of initial evidence that past life experiences impact these types of repetitive behaviors,” said one behavioral neuroscientist.
Biology Letters Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Another way of keeping time
Jellies and their relatives can’t track time like most other animals; they somehow lost key genes involved in circadian clocks. But researchers serendipitously discovered that at least one pea-sized jelly has a totally different timekeeping system. The findings suggest “systems like this might be much more widespread,” and we’re simply not looking for them, said one chronobiologist.
PLOS Biology Paper  |  Read more at Quanta Magazine
Pee or get off the tree
A team of ecologists happened upon a communal forest latrine when looking to place a camera. “It was crazy,” one of the researchers said. “It is almost the total number of canopy mammals that you can find in the cloud forest.”
Ecology and Evolution Paper  |  Read more at New Scientist
Last but not least
Today, I’m drawing inspiration from resurrection plants: True resilience often comes from letting go of the very thing you’re most afraid of losing.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Ana Georgescu, Perri Thaler, and Hannah Richter

Do you have a burning science question you can’t seem to find a good answer for? Submit it to Ask Science! Selected questions will receive responses from Science editors right here in ScienceAdviser.

raw_metadata.subject: Asking ChatGPT if you’re a jerk could make you more of one
